insights: Strong gravitational pull toward AI-native developer tooling — building systems that compress the mechanical parts of engineering
Infrastructure cost optimization is an underrated skill. The $300/day → $500/month reduction at Absinthe wasn't just containerization — it was rethinking what actually needs to run
Multi-tenancy done right lives in the database, not the application. RLS at Bloom eliminated an entire class of bugs
Local-first AI is a design philosophy, not just a deployment choice. Wispr Flow proves that privacy and performance aren't in tension
The gap between 'working on my machine' and 'running in production' is where most builders get stuck. Neploy was an attempt to close it
Agent orchestration works best when agents are specialized. Monolithic LLM calls fail at complex tasks. Decomposition is the answer
patterns: Every significant project involves building a data pipeline — ingestion, transformation, and serving are the universal backend pattern
Real-time systems (WebSockets, Kafka, Realtime) show up in nearly every role. The ability to build systems that react instantly is a core competency
Cloud infrastructure (AWS, Docker, Terraform) is the substrate. The interesting work happens on top, but the substrate has to be solid
Projects succeed when onboarding is obsessed over. Bloom's 12-iteration onboarding flow proved this. Technical excellence means nothing if users can't get started
The best architectures are modular enough to iterate on independently. Tight coupling kills velocity
